comment_content:
Cunha holds the ball for far too long and constantly kills the attacking flow. He looks for his own goal before the team, ignoring simple passing options when they’re clearly there. This isn’t confidence — it’s selfishness. As a result, the striker is always isolated and attacks end as one-man actions.
Look at Man City or Arsenal. If you don’t play combinations, you’re benched. Even Haaland plays one-touch football for the team. Cunha does the opposite — he chases highlights instead of combinations and breaks the team’s rhythm every time.

comment_content:
It's not a big win but a narrow one.
Man  U is still a one half team as shown below:
1. Consistency is still an issue, and the team had a few disappointing results in November/December, such as a home draw against West Ham and a loss to Aston Villa. 
2. Man U played for over 80 minutes with an extra man on home ground but failed to score or even secure a draw against Everton. This specific game is evidence that tactical issues and a lack of fighting spirit persist, undermining arguments of consistent improvement. It was a historic loss for United, as it was the first time in 47 Premier League games that a visiting team won at Old Trafford after having a player sent off.
